patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 58-year-old male high school science teacher (college-educated). You are cooperative and communicate clearly. Start with: “My right hand has been shaking, and I feel slower than I used to.” Early in the visit, volunteer: right-hand tremor at rest (worse with stress/tiredness), moving slower (dressing takes longer), handwriting smaller/messier, and that your dad had a similar shaking/movement problem. If asked about other symptoms, mention quieter/monotone voice, morning shoulder/neck stiffness, reduced smell, constipation. Be hesitant/embarrassed and disclose only if directly asked about urinary/sexual issues: urgency/leakage and reduced libido/performance. Express misconceptions: you worried it’s anxiety, essential tremor, or a pinched nerve. If clinician shows empathy, acknowledge it and open up gradually. Raise cost/insurance concerns about genetic testing, therapy copays, and expensive treatments.

tool_call_sequence.description: Correctly identifies Parkinson disease as the primary diagnosis for an L1 (diagnosis) task; enables appropriate counseling and follow-up planning without unnecessary prescribing.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as "Parkinson disease" and explains it in patient-friendly terms (a neurodegenerative movement disorder related to dopamine deficiency in the basal ganglia).

Includes the required (even if atypical) inheritance phrasing by mentioning "autosomal dominant" during diagnosis explanation, while avoiding misleading certainty (e.g., clarifies that most Parkinson disease is sporadic and that some familial forms can be autosomal dominant).
Elicits core supporting features for Parkinson disease: bradykinesia plus at least one of rest tremor or rigidity, and asks about symptom asymmetry and progression.

Assesses functional impact (ADLs, gait/falls, work/driving) and screens for common non-motor symptoms (constipation, REM sleep behavior disorder, depression/anxiety, cognitive changes, anosmia, orthostatic symptoms).
Checks medication history for dopamine-blocking agents that can mimic/worsen parkinsonism (e.g., antipsychotics, metoclopramide) and documents avoidance of metoclopramide in counseling per milestone.
Does not prescribe medications in an L1 diagnosis-only benchmark; if discussing treatment, frames it as education and shared decision-making rather than issuing a prescription.
If treatment is discussed, mentions "carbidopa-levodopa" as standard symptomatic therapy and gives high-level expectations/side effects without initiating therapy via tool use.

Identifies red flags for atypical parkinsonism and indicates need for further evaluation if present (early falls, early severe autonomic failure, vertical gaze palsy, rapid progression, poor levodopa response, early dementia/hallucinations, prominent cerebellar signs).

Maintains safety-check awareness: before any future prescribing, states intent to perform allergy, contraindication, and drug-interaction checks (critical checks), even though no prescription is made in this task.
Provides a clear follow-up recommendation (timing and what to monitor) and advises urgent evaluation for acute neurologic deficits, repeated falls with injury, syncope, or sudden marked worsening.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose Parkinson disease based on classic motor features and provide basic counseling including required milestones, without prescribing.
steps: Ask about slowness of movement (bradykinesia) and at least one of rest tremor or rigidity; clarify laterality/asymmetry and course.
Screen for medication-induced parkinsonism contributors (especially dopamine antagonists) and explicitly counsel to avoid metoclopramide.
Explain the diagnosis using the term "Parkinson disease" and include the phrase "autosomal dominant" with appropriate clarification about sporadic vs familial forms.
Briefly explain treatment options including "carbidopa-levodopa" as standard symptomatic therapy (education only; no prescription).
Record the diagnosis using record_diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Adds non-motor symptom screening and functional assessment before recording diagnosis; still diagnosis-only with counseling.
steps: Elicit motor symptoms (bradykinesia, rest tremor/rigidity), asymmetry, progression; ask about gait freezing and falls.
Assess non-motor symptoms (constipation, sleep disturbance/REM behavior, mood, cognition, orthostasis) and functional impact (ADLs, driving, work).
Review medications for dopamine antagonists; advise avoiding metoclopramide.
Explain "Parkinson disease" and include "autosomal dominant" with nuance; discuss carbidopa-levodopa as a common treatment option without prescribing.
Use record_diagnosis to document Parkinson disease.

acceptable_variants.description: Documents a brief differential before committing to Parkinson disease, then records the final diagnosis (differential is discussed in NL only; tool use remains diagnosis-only).
steps: Gather key motor features and timing; ask about exposure to dopamine-blocking drugs and stroke history.
Verbally consider differential (essential tremor, drug-induced parkinsonism) and identify why Parkinson disease is most consistent (rest tremor, bradykinesia, rigidity, asymmetry).
Provide required counseling: explain Parkinson disease; include "autosomal dominant" with clarification; mention carbidopa-levodopa; avoid metoclopramide.
Record Parkinson disease with record_diagnosis.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as "Sinding-Larsen-Johansson disease" and links it to overuse/traction at the patellar tendon insertion (inferior patellar pole) in a growing adolescent.
Explains that this is typically self-limited and improves with conservative care; avoids alarming language (e.g., fracture, tumor) unless red flags are present.
Explains treatment including activity modification (relative rest from provoking jumping/running/sports, gradual return) and NSAIDs for short-term pain control if appropriate.
Provides practical conservative measures: ice after activity, stretching/strengthening (quadriceps/hamstrings), consider patellar strap/taping, and physical therapy if persistent symptoms.

Screens for red flags that would change management (e.g., fever, night pain, significant swelling/effusion, locking/instability, inability to bear weight, acute trauma, rapidly worsening pain) and recommends urgent evaluation/imaging if present.
If discussing medications, performs required safety checks before prescribing: checks contraindications to NSAIDs (critical), and also checks allergies and potential drug interactions (non-critical).
Sets expectations for follow-up and return-to-sport guidance (pain-guided, stepwise increase; reassess if not improving within a few weeks).
Uses clear, age-appropriate communication and engages patient/parent in shared plan (what to stop/modify, what is safe to continue, when to return).
